quote: Biometric data converts to encrypted templates in binary code, not stored images. Templates cannot be reverse-engineered to reveal original fingerprints.
why: Objectively false present-day statement, stated as an unconditional technical fact rather than as 2020 opinion. More damagingly, it actively undermines what AmbiSecure sells today: the entire commercial argument for OnePass Bio Card and BioKey is that the template must never leave the secure element. That argument only has force if templates are sensitive — i.e. if they CAN be inverted. This page tells the buyer the thing AmbiSecure charges a premium to protect is not worth protecting. The 2026 box ("never ship biometric templates off the secure element") asserts the correct architecture but does not retract the claim that makes it unnecessary.
evidence: Cappelli, Lumini, Maio & Maltoni, "Fingerprint Image Reconstruction from Standard Templates", IEEE TPAMI 29(9), 2007 — reconstructs matchable fingerprint images from ISO/ANSI minutiae templates. ISO/IEC 24745 and ISO/IEC 30136 exist specifically because irreversibility must be engineered into a template protection scheme, not assumed. GDPR Art. 9 / India DPDP treat biometric templates as sensitive personal data precisely on inversion risk. OFFERING-MATRIX: "template never leaves silicon" (OnePass Bio Card), "match-on-device" (BioKey).
recommendation: Fix — correct to the engineered-property framing, which strengthens rather than weakens the product story.
proposed_wording: Biometric data converts to encrypted templates in binary code, not stored images. A template is not the original image, but irreversibility is a property that has to be engineered and protected — research has reconstructed matchable fingerprints from standard templates, which is why the template must stay inside the secure element and never be exported.

The 2026 box addresses on-device matching and template handling but says nothing about presentation attacks or coercion, so no context on the page resolves it. Note the first clause ("eliminate certain vulnerabilities") is properly hedged — only the second clause is defective.
evidence: ISO/IEC 30107 (presentation attack detection) exists because biometric capture from photographs, latent prints and lifted samples is a recognised attack class. Chaos Computer Club demonstrated iris spoofing from a photograph (2017) and fingerprint spoofing from a high-resolution photograph of a subject's hand (2014). Coerced/compelled unlock is a well-documented real-world path. Deepfake voice and face injection attacks are now a mainstream fraud vector.
recommendation: Fix — soften "cannot be tricked" to the accurate property (biometrics are not disclosable by persuasion, but are capturable and spoofable).
proposed_wording: Biometrics eliminate certain vulnerabilities associated with passwords and PINs — a user cannot be talked into disclosing a fingerprint the way they can be talked into disclosing a password. They remain exposed to presentation attacks and coerced unlock, which is why liveness detection and on-device matching matter.

quote: Combining single sign-on with MFA will strengthen security and stop hackers in their path. Even with a compromised password, they would not be able to access user accounts and systems.
why: Absolute claim ("stop hackers in their path", "would not be able to access") that is objectively false as a present-day statement and, critically, it undermines AmbiSecure's central commercial differentiator. The sentence says ANY MFA plus SSO stops attackers — which flattens exactly the distinction AmbiSecure sells on: phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2, PIV) versus possession-only MFA (SMS, push). If any MFA is sufficient, there is no reason to buy hardware. The page's own 2026 box gestures at AAL2/AAL3 policy but does not retract the claim.
evidence: The site's sibling archive page /blog/archive/common-misconceptions-about-2fa/ 2026 box states the opposite: "enterprises now distinguish phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2, PIV) from possession-only MFA (SMS, push)". /solutions/phishing-resistant-authentication/index.html:152: "no single control eliminates all account compromise". Adversary-in-the-middle phishing kits (Evilginx2, EvilProxy) proxy non-phishing-resistant MFA in real time; MFA-fatigue/push-bombing defeated push MFA in the 2022 Uber and Cisco breaches. OMB M-22-09 mandates phishing-resistant MFA precisely because generic MFA is insufficient.
recommendation: Fix — keep the SSO+MFA recommendation but restore the factor-strength distinction the business depends on.
proposed_wording: Combining single sign-on with MFA materially strengthens security: even with a compromised password, an attacker still has to defeat the second factor. How much that is worth depends on the factor — SMS and push MFA can be proxied or fatigued, whereas phishing-resistant factors such as FIDO2 and PIV bind to the origin and cannot be relayed.
validation_result: file no longer a live page

quote: A passwordless authentication system is one that swaps the use of a traditional password with more secure factors. These extra-security methods may include a magic link, fingerprint, PIN, or a token delivered via email or text message.
why: Presents emailed and SMS-delivered tokens as "more secure factors" — dated guidance stated as a current definition, and it directly contradicts three other things on this same site. It contradicts the sibling archive post whose entire thesis is that SMS OTP is indefensible; it contradicts this page's own 2026 box ("FIDO2 + passkeys have become the production answer"); and it undermines what AmbiSecure sells, since a magic link is phishable and an SMS token is the exact factor NIST removed. This is the definitional paragraph of the page, so it frames everything after it.
evidence: Sibling page /blog/archive/sms-otp-disadvantages/ 2026 box: "NIST SP 800-63-4 has finalised the removal of SMS for AAL2+ ... SMS OTP now survives only as a recovery method on consumer services." NIST SP 800-63B restricts out-of-band SMS; SP 800-63-4 removes it for AAL2+. OFFERING-MATRIX core position: "Passwordless / FIDO2 / WebAuthn; device-bound credentials (BE=0) for high assurance".
recommendation: Fix — keep the 2021 enumeration but mark the weak factors rather than endorsing them as "more secure".
proposed_wording: A passwordless authentication system is one that swaps the traditional password for a factor the user does not have to remember or retype. In 2021 that label covered a wide range — magic links, fingerprint, PIN, or a token delivered via email or text message. Only some of these are phishing-resistant: emailed and SMS-delivered tokens can be relayed to an attacker, which is why the model settled on hardware-bound FIDO2 credentials.
validation_result: file no longer a live page

quote: On-device biometric processing keeps data local rather than cloud-stored, eliminating centralized database risks while maintaining GDPR compliance.
why: Two defects. "eliminating centralized database risks" is an absolute claim. "maintaining GDPR compliance" is an unsupported regulatory claim: an architecture does not confer compliance — lawful basis, explicit consent for Art. 9 special-category data, DPIA, retention and data-subject rights all still apply regardless of where matching happens. Stated flatly on a page selling into the workplace, this is the kind of sentence a buyer's DPO will quote back.
evidence: GDPR Art. 9(1) classes biometric data used for unique identification as special-category data requiring an Art. 9(2) condition; Art. 35 requires a DPIA for large-scale special-category processing — none of which on-device matching discharges. OFFERING-MATRIX claim rules: no absolute claims; "/about/certifications and /trust say alignment/targeted, NOT awarded", i.e. the site's own posture is to claim alignment, never conferred compliance.
recommendation: Fix — downgrade to the supportable claim (reduces risk, supports compliance).
proposed_wording: On-device biometric processing keeps data local rather than cloud-stored, removing the centralised template database as a breach target and making GDPR obligations substantially easier to discharge — though lawful basis, consent and DPIA duties still apply.
validation_result: file no longer a live page

quote: Reality: Poor implementation (like fingerprint-only systems) creates problems. Location-based authentication demonstrates effective, user-friendly 2FA. The cost of data breaches far exceeds implementation expenses.
why: Dated technical guidance presented as current, under a heading literally labelled "Reality". Location is not an authentication factor — it is a risk signal — so "location-based authentication" is not 2FA at all, and endorsing it undermines AmbiSecure's hardware-possession position. The clause also disparages "fingerprint-only systems" as "poor implementation" in the same breath, which cuts against OnePass Bio Card and BioKey. The 2026 box distinguishes phishing-resistant from possession-only MFA but never addresses location.
evidence: NIST SP 800-63B §4 defines exactly three factors (something you know / have / are) and explicitly excludes geolocation as an authentication factor, permitting it only as a risk-based signal. Geolocation is trivially spoofed via VPN/GPS spoofing and cannot bind an authentication event. OFFERING-MATRIX core positions: "Smart cards and hardware tokens as a STRENGTH"; on-card fingerprint match is a headline OnePass Bio Card feature.
recommendation: Fix — replace the location endorsement and remove the swipe at fingerprint-only systems.
proposed_wording: Reality: Poor implementation creates problems, not 2FA itself. Location is a useful risk signal but is not an authentication factor and cannot stand in for one. A hardware possession factor — a security key or smart card — delivers strong, low-friction 2FA in a single tap. The cost of data breaches far exceeds implementation expenses.

The 2026 box mentions EMV Contactless Transit Specification maturity but does not correct the version.

Note the same page's "EMVCo, composed of six member organizations" is still accurate (Amex, Discover, JCB, Mastercard, UnionPay, Visa) and needs no change.

proposed_wording: The specifications began in 1994 through collaboration between Europay, MasterCard, and Visa. JCB joined in 2004, and American Express in 2009. Version EMV '96 was released in 1996, with production version 3.1.1 in 1998. Version 4.3 followed in 2011, and EMVCo published version 4.4 in 2022.

A buyer relying on this in a threat model is being told the residual risk is zero when the certification actually quantifies it as non-zero.

quote: A 9,000-seat engineering and operations workforce migrating from SMS one-time codes and TOTP apps to FIDO2 hardware authenticators — against a legacy stack that included Okta SSO for SaaS, Microsoft Entra ID for Windows, and a CAS-backed internal application portal that predated WebAuthn by a decade.
why: None of the three individual case-study pages carries an anonymisation notice — the disclosure exists only on the /case-studies/ index. A reader arriving directly from search or a shared link (the normal path for a case study) sees specific, checkable-sounding facts — 9,000 seats, named third-party products, '12% of staff were on SMS', 'approximately 40 line-of-business applications', 'before their next ISO/IEC 27001 surveillance audit' — narrated throughout as 'the customer', with nothing on the page stating that the engagement is anonymised or that figures are illustrative. That reads as a real, named-but-withheld reference account. If these numbers are composites or illustrative rather than drawn from an actual engagement, the pages are unmarked fabrications on a surface buyers weigh heavily. The index's own promise makes it worse, not better.
evidence: grep for 'anonymis|anonymiz|illustrative|composite' returns 0 matches in all three case-study detail pages; only case-studies/index.html matches. case-studies/index.html states: "Anonymised architecture write-ups from real engagements. Each one focuses on the deployment constraint that shaped the design — not on who the customer was. Customer names and metrics are kept private; the engineering is not." Note the internal tension: the index promises metrics are kept private, while the detail pages publish 9,000 seats (passwordless-workforce), '700+ validator gates' and 'Sub-300 ms tap-and-go' (transit-ticketing), and 'tens of thousands per year' (secure-device-identity). Same pattern on all three: transit-ticketing/index.html — "A metropolitan transit operator rolling out closed-loop DESFire EV2 ticketing across 700+ validator gates"; secure-device-identity/index.html — "A fleet-class IoT manufacturer adding hardware-backed device identity to a connected industrial product line".
recommendation: Add a standing disclosure block to each of the three detail pages, directly under the dek, matching the index's language — e.g. "Anonymised: this write-up describes a real engagement. The customer is not named, and figures are rounded or altered where they could identify the deployment. The architecture is described as built." If any page is in fact illustrative rather than a real engagement, say so explicitly and drop 'real engagements'/'real deployments' from the index copy and the CollectionPage schema description. Also reconcile 'metrics are kept private' on the index with the metrics the detail pages publish — the honest version is 'customer names are withheld and identifying figures are rounded'.

quote: Over the decades the team has shipped Bluetooth-based hardware (the Hedron project for Ciright), wearable platforms for Actofit, biometric + GSM security devices for CEEL, prosthetic-control electronics for Robo Bionics, and tens of thousands of smart labels for retail and logistics.
why: Names four customers (Ciright, Actofit, CEEL, Robo Bionics) plus a deployment count ('tens of thousands of smart labels') on a page that sits under the AmbiSecure brand, while the Trust Center states AmbiSecure does not disclose named customers and the case-studies index states customer names are kept private. Two distinct problems. (1) Consent: named customer disclosure normally requires written permission; nothing on the page indicates it was obtained. (2) Brand confusion — the audit's explicit concern: these are Ambimat Electronics engagements (PCB/firmware/wearables/prosthetics), not AmbiSecure security engagements, but the sentence's subject is 'the team' on a page titled 'About AmbiSecure'. A buyer evaluating AmbiSecure's security track record will read Ciright/Actofit/CEEL/Robo Bionics as AmbiSecure security references. None of them is a security deployment.
evidence: trust/index.html: "What we explicitly do NOT claim: AmbiSecure ... does not disclose named customers or specific city deployments on the public site." case-studies/index.html: "Customer names and metrics are kept private; the engineering is not." OFFERING-MATRIX.md: "No customer names, deployment counts, market-leadership or performance figures unless the authoritative page states them." The same about page separates the entities correctly elsewhere — "AmbiSecure is the security business unit of Ambimat Electronics" — so the standard exists; this sentence breaches it.
recommendation: Confirm written permission from each named firm; drop any without it. Regardless of permission, re-attribute explicitly to the parent so the security track record is not overstated: "Before AmbiSecure was carved out, Ambimat Electronics shipped Bluetooth hardware, wearable platforms, biometric + GSM security devices, prosthetic-control electronics, and smart labels for retail and logistics — the embedded-hardware depth AmbiSecure was built on." Drop 'tens of thousands' unless it can be evidenced.

quote: There are none. AmbiSecure secure elements are sourced from CC EAL6+ certified silicon vendors. The applet code is signed by the issuer, not by AmbiSecure. There is no extractable per-device master key, no remote update channel that bypasses the issuer, and no escrow of user credentials.
why: Answering 'What about government backdoors or master keys in AmbiSecure devices?' with an unqualified 'There are none.' asserts a universal negative across silicon AmbiSecure does not manufacture and cannot fully audit. AmbiSecure can speak authoritatively for its own applet layer and its own key-handling; it cannot certify the absence of vendor- or state-level backdoors in third-party chips. As written, the strongest possible security assurance is extended over a supply chain the company's own certifications page is careful to disclaim responsibility for. Note the following sentence quietly concedes the point by pointing at the chip vendor's reports rather than AmbiSecure's own.
evidence: about/certifications/index.html: "Chip-level evaluation does not auto-extend to the applet layer. A CC EAL6+ chip-platform certificate is held by the silicon vendor and applies to the chip platform; AmbiSecure-authored applets sitting on that platform are not separately evaluated unless explicitly stated." trust/index.html scopes disclosure out of AmbiSecure's control for exactly this boundary: "What is out of scope: ... physical attacks against silicon partners’ supply chain (refer to those vendors directly)." OFFERING-MATRIX.md: "No absolute claims".
recommendation: Scope the answer to what AmbiSecure controls and can evidence: "None that AmbiSecure implements or has access to. Our applets contain no backdoor, no master key, and no credential escrow; applet code is signed by the issuer, not by AmbiSecure, and there is no remote update channel that bypasses the issuer. For the silicon itself, we source from CC EAL6+ certified vendors whose evaluation reports are public — the certification measures resistance against a defined attack potential, and we can disclose chip-vendor identities under NDA for supply-chain audit." Mirror into the JSON-LD copy at line 104.

quote: Is the AmbiSecure card waterproof and durable enough for daily use? ... Yes. The card body is ISO/IEC 7810 ID-1 standard plastic and survives normal wallet conditions — rain, sweat, brief immersion.
why: Answers a direct 'is it waterproof' question with an unqualified 'Yes' and cites ISO/IEC 7810 ID-1 as if it supported the claim. ISO/IEC 7810 ID-1 is a dimensional and physical-characteristics standard for card bodies — it is not a waterproofing or ingress-protection standard, so the citation does not evidence the answer. No IP rating is given. 'survives... brief immersion' is a specific durability claim with nothing behind it, and it invites warranty claims from users who immerse a card and lose it. Uses the legacy 'AmbiSecure card' name as well.
evidence: about/certifications/index.html lists ISO/IEC 7810 ID-1 under "Smart card / contactless" among "Standards we build against", and states plainly: "Standards conformance is not certification. Building to a standard (e.g. ISO/IEC 7816-4) is not the same as being certified against an evaluation scheme." No IP rating appears anywhere in the repo for the card body.
recommendation: Requalify: "The card body is standard ISO/IEC 7810 ID-1 polycarbonate/PVC and handles normal wallet conditions — rain and sweat. It is not IP-rated, so treat sustained immersion as out of spec. USB-key variants are ABS / metal-shell and survive keychain wear. The secure element inside has no battery, no firmware to die, and no moving parts." Retitle the question to 'How durable is the card?' and rename 'AmbiSecure card' to 'OnePass Card'. Mirror into the JSON-LD copy at line 72.
